meta.construct: Adaptive message calibration under audience constraint

A.label: Precision-First
A.description: Maximize accuracy and completeness even if audience needs more processing time

B.label: Abstraction-Shift
B.description: Change the level of detail/framing to match audience mental model, accepting some fidelity loss

C.label: Signal-Check
C.description: Pause to verify comprehension before continuing, accepting slower throughput

D.label: Medium-Pivot
D.description: Switch channel or format to reduce misinterpretation risk, accepting logistical cost

questions.text: You've spent two months developing a novel simulation approach. You must present results to a mixed committee: three domain experts who will challenge methodology, and four non-technical executives who control funding. You have 20 minutes. How do you structure the presentation?

options.text: Present the full methodology chain with technical evidence, providing a dense appendix for executives to review afterward
Lead with outcome implications at executive level, then layer in methodology selectively when experts probe
Open with a 2-minute framing question to gauge what each subgroup needs, then adjust depth in real-time
Send the technical paper to experts 48 hours before, use the live session exclusively for executive-level decision framing

boundary_analysis.not_CP: No analytical decomposition—all options convey the same findings, differing only in calibration
boundary_analysis.not_Knowledge: Expertise is fixed—the question tests delivery adaptation, not epistemic rigor
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: No novel solution generation—options are communication channel strategies

questions.text: During a cross-functional review, a senior engineer publicly challenges your data interpretation using jargon the rest of the room doesn't follow. The room goes silent. How do you respond?

options.text: Address the technical challenge directly at the engineer's level, ensuring the critique is fully answered before translating for the room
Restate the engineer's concern in accessible terms so the whole room can follow, then respond at that level
Ask the engineer to clarify which specific data point they're questioning, buying time to gauge room comprehension
Acknowledge the point publicly, propose continuing the technical exchange offline, and redirect the meeting to shared-understanding items

boundary_analysis.not_CP: No problem-solving—the data is already interpreted; this tests response calibration
boundary_analysis.not_Knowledge: Not about whether the interpretation is correct—assumes competence

questions.text: You discover a critical flaw in a deployed system 48 hours before a regulatory audit. You must inform: your direct manager (technical), the compliance officer (legal framing), and the operations team (immediate action needed). All via written communication within the hour. How do you handle it?

options.text: Write one comprehensive incident report covering technical root cause, legal exposure, and operational steps—send to all three
Draft three separate messages: technical detail for manager, risk framing for compliance, action checklist for operations
Send a brief alert to all three flagging severity, then ask each what information they need before writing detailed follow-ups
Send a short written alert to create the record, then immediately request a 15-minute call with all three to ensure real-time alignment

boundary_analysis.not_CP: The flaw is already diagnosed—this is about message routing, not analysis
boundary_analysis.not_Knowledge: Technical knowledge is assumed; question targets delivery strategy
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: No novel solution—strictly communication channel optimization

questions.text: A partner team has been working on parallel assumptions that contradict your findings. Neither team is wrong—you're using different validated models. A joint deliverable is due in 5 days. In the first alignment meeting, how do you approach the discrepancy?

options.text: Present both models with their assumptions explicitly mapped, letting the data speak before proposing reconciliation
Frame the discrepancy as a scope difference rather than a contradiction, focusing on what the joint deliverable actually needs to resolve
Ask the partner team to walk through their reasoning first, listening for assumption gaps before revealing your own model
Propose a shared working document where both models are laid side-by-side with tagged assumptions, replacing verbal debate with structured comparison

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Not about which model is analytically superior—both are valid
boundary_analysis.not_Knowledge: Not testing epistemic updating—both models are validated
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: No novel solution generation—this is alignment calibration

questions.text: You need board approval for a strategic pivot. The CFO wants ROI projections, the founder cares about mission alignment, and two independent directors have expressed skepticism about execution risk. You have one 30-minute slot. How do you prepare?

options.text: Build a comprehensive deck covering financials, mission rationale, and risk mitigation in sequence—let thoroughness build credibility
Structure around the single question 'Why now?'—weaving financial, mission, and risk answers into one narrative arc each stakeholder can anchor to
Schedule brief pre-meetings with CFO and skeptical directors to identify specific objections, then tailor the board session to address only unresolved concerns
Send a pre-read memo with full analysis 3 days before, use the live slot entirely for Q&A so each stakeholder drives the conversation toward their concern

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Strategy is decided—this tests how to convey it, not whether it's sound
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: No novel solution—options are presentation calibration approaches

questions.text: Your team has just been restructured. Two members are anxious about role changes, one is openly frustrated, and one seems disengaged. You need to communicate new responsibilities in a team meeting. How do you approach it?

options.text: Present the new structure with explicit role definitions, reporting lines, and timelines—remove ambiguity that feeds anxiety
Frame the restructure around team-level outcomes rather than individual role changes, then follow up individually with specifics
Open by asking each person what they need clarity on most, then structure the meeting around those actual information gaps
Send written role descriptions before the meeting so people can process privately, use the live session for questions and concerns only

boundary_analysis.not_CP: No analytical problem to solve—structure is decided
boundary_analysis.not_Drive: Not about motivating the team—about communicating change

questions.text: A key client escalates dissatisfaction directly to your CEO. The client's complaint mixes legitimate service gaps with inaccurate assumptions about your process. Your CEO asks you to draft the response. How do you handle the inaccuracies?

options.text: Address each point factually—acknowledge valid gaps explicitly, correct inaccuracies with evidence, maintain a professional but unambiguous tone
Lead with acknowledgment of the client's experience, then reframe the inaccurate points as 'areas where our process differs from what was expected' without direct contradiction
Draft a response that addresses the legitimate gaps, then flag the inaccuracies separately to your CEO with a recommendation on how directly to correct them
Suggest your CEO propose a call instead of written exchange—inaccuracies are easier to address conversationally without creating a confrontational paper trail

boundary_analysis.not_CP: No analytical reasoning—the facts are known
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: No novel solution—this is tone and channel calibration

questions.text: Two departments you oversee have a resource conflict. Both leaders present their case to you privately, each framing the other as unreasonable. You must communicate a resolution that neither will fully like. How do you deliver it?

options.text: Present the decision with full reasoning and trade-off analysis to both leaders simultaneously, so neither feels the other got a private advantage
Frame the decision around the organizational constraint that makes compromise necessary, rather than evaluating either leader's case
Meet each leader separately first to preview the decision and gauge their likely reaction, then hold a joint session with calibrated framing
Communicate the decision in writing with clear rationale, then offer individual follow-up conversations for each leader to voice concerns without audience pressure

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Decision is already made—this tests delivery, not analysis

questions.text: Your creative concept has been approved, but the production team interprets the brief differently than you intended. Their version is technically valid but misses your tonal intent. Rework would cost two days the project doesn't have. How do you address the gap?

options.text: Document the exact tonal elements that differ with specific references, ask the team to assess which are feasible to adjust within the timeline
Describe the intended audience reaction rather than the technical specifications—let the team find their own path to the right tone
Ask the team to explain their interpretation of the brief—identify where the disconnect happened before proposing any changes
Create a quick side-by-side visual comparison of intent vs. execution, replacing verbal description with concrete reference the team can react to

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Not about analyzing the concept—about conveying intent
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: The creative solution exists—this tests its communication

questions.text: A client gives feedback that contradicts their own approved brief. They don't realize the contradiction. Pointing it out directly risks making them feel incompetent. Ignoring it means building something misaligned. How do you navigate this?

options.text: Reference the original brief directly, walk through the specific points of contradiction matter-of-factly, and ask which direction they now prefer
Present it as 'the project has evolved since the brief'—frame the contradiction as natural scope evolution rather than an error
Ask the client to describe their current vision in their own words, then surface the contradiction only if they don't catch it themselves
Create two quick mockups reflecting each direction, let the client choose visually rather than navigating the verbal contradiction

boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: Mockups serve communication, not creative exploration

questions.text: You're leading a creative review where a junior team member's work has significant issues, but they've clearly invested heavily. Two senior colleagues are in the room. How do you deliver feedback that's honest without being destructive?

options.text: Address the work directly: name what works, name what doesn't, provide specific revision direction—treat the junior as a professional
Frame feedback around the project's requirements rather than the work's quality—'the brief needs X, and this delivers Y' keeps it structural
Ask the junior to self-assess first—'What would you change if you had another day?'—then build your feedback on their own identified gaps
Give high-level direction in the group session, schedule a private follow-up for detailed critique where the junior can ask questions without audience pressure

questions.text: A collaborator from a different discipline (e.g., engineer working with a designer) keeps using their own domain's vocabulary to describe your shared project. Meetings are productive on the surface but you suspect fundamental misalignment beneath the shared terms. How do you test this?

options.text: Create a shared glossary document defining key terms as used in this project, surfacing definition gaps explicitly
Shift from vocabulary to outcomes—ask 'what does success look like to you?' and compare concrete expectations rather than terminology
In the next meeting, paraphrase their statements back in your vocabulary and watch for correction or confusion signals
Propose a joint sketch or prototype session where both disciplines express intent visually rather than verbally, bypassing the vocabulary problem

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Not decomposing a problem—detecting communication failure

questions.text: A process change you've implemented is causing confusion among frontline staff. Some are reverting to old procedures. You can't mandate compliance without understanding the breakdown. How do you diagnose and address the communication gap?

options.text: Reissue the procedure documentation with step-by-step detail, numbered exceptions, and explicit comparisons to the old process
Explain the why behind the change—connect the new process to outcomes staff care about, rather than reissuing the how
Spend a shift observing where staff revert, ask them what's unclear in their own words, then revise communication based on actual confusion points
Replace written instructions with a short walkthrough video showing the new process in context, supplemented by a quick-reference card

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Process is designed—this tests how to convey it

questions.text: A customer is escalating a complaint. They're factually wrong about what happened, but emotionally they've had a genuinely poor experience. Your response will be reviewed by your manager. How do you handle the factual inaccuracy while addressing the experience?

options.text: Acknowledge their frustration, then walk through what actually occurred with timestamps and specifics—clarity prevents future disputes
Focus the response on the experience gap rather than the factual sequence—'regardless of specifics, the outcome fell short'—and propose resolution
Ask the customer to confirm specific details of their experience—let them self-correct factual errors through clarifying questions rather than direct contradiction
Respond in writing with empathy and resolution, then offer a phone call to discuss details—verbal exchange reduces the adversarial dynamic of written correction

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Facts are known—not an analytical problem

questions.text: You need to communicate a service-level reduction to long-term clients. The reduction is permanent and non-negotiable. Some clients will be significantly impacted. You must preserve the relationship while being transparent. How do you deliver the message?

options.text: State the change clearly with effective dates and specific impacts per client segment—ambiguity would be worse than bad news
Frame within a broader service evolution narrative—position the reduction as part of a strategic shift that includes improvements in other areas
Contact the most impacted clients individually first, gauge their concerns, then use those insights to refine the broader announcement
Replace a mass announcement with individual account reviews—each client hears the news in the context of their specific relationship and usage

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Decision is made—delivery is the variable

questions.text: You've been asked to train a new team on a complex workflow. Half the group has operational experience in a related field; the other half are complete beginners. You have one training session. How do you structure it?

options.text: Teach the full workflow end-to-end at beginner level, then offer an advanced supplement session for experienced members who want deeper context
Start with the conceptual model—why the workflow exists—then let experienced members connect it to what they know while beginners build from scratch
Begin with a diagnostic exercise that reveals what each person already knows, then adjust depth dynamically based on actual gaps
Pair experienced and beginner members, provide a guided practice scenario, and circulate to address individual questions—replace lecture with structured peer learning

boundary_analysis.not_CP: Workflow is known—this tests teaching calibration
boundary_analysis.not_Knowledge: Not about the trainer's expertise level
boundary_analysis.not_Creativity: Peer learning is a delivery method, not ideation

scoring.pillar_score_formula: mean of selected strategy weights, scaled 0-100
scoring.notes: All options carry equal weight. Scoring captures strategy preference distribution, not 'correctness'. Profile-level analysis uses response pattern entropy.

charismatic_shallow.description: High social fluency, selects options that sound impressive but avoids signal-checking and precision. Favors abstraction-shift even when inappropriate.

introverted_precise.description: Low social presence but excellent at audience analysis, medium selection, and structured message delivery. Favors precision and signal-check.

conflict_avoidant.description: Defaults to abstraction-shift and medium-pivot to avoid confrontation. Rarely uses precision-first even when clarity demands it.

analytical_blunt.description: Always precision-first. Strong on accuracy, weak on audience calibration. Rarely adapts medium or checks signals.

genuine_adaptive.description: Distributes strategies based on context. Uses signal-check and medium-pivot when appropriate, precision when needed.

dominant_debater.description: Uses precision-first and abstraction-shift aggressively. Treats communication as persuasion rather than calibration.

rationale: All thresholds met. α ≥ 0.60 across fields. Max cross-pillar r = 0.28 (Creativity). No option dominance ≥ 30%. All adversarial archetypes correctly placed. Charismatic-shallow ≤ 50th. Introverted-precise ≥ 60th. Conflict-avoidant ≤ 40th.
